data.question: If fraudulent misrepresentation is discovered after 5 years, what happens?

options.A: Insurer must pay, contestability period expired
options.B: Insurer can void policy entirely, fraud invalidates contestability limit
options.C: Benefit is adjusted for age error only
options.D: 10% penalty is assessed on death benefit

data.explanation: They can void the policy entirely and deny the claim, because fraud invalidates the 2-year contestability limit
data.kidExplanation: The insurance company has a 2-year safety window for honest mistakes. But if you lied ON PURPOSE to trick them, that safety window disappears! They can rip up your contract even 5 years later.

data.question: What is an Attending Physician Statement (APS)?

options.B: Report from applicant's doctor detailing medical history

data.explanation: A report written by the applicant's personal doctor detailing their medical history, requested by the underwriter
data.kidExplanation: An APS is just a fancy letter the insurance company asks your personal doctor to write. The letter tells the company exactly how healthy you are and what medicines you take!

data.question: Why might a stay-at-home parent need life insurance?

options.B: To cover caregiving replacement expenses like childcare
options.D: Only if they have high income

data.explanation: Because replacing their caregiving duties (e.g., childcare, household management) will result in significant new expenses for the surviving spouse
data.kidExplanation: Even if mom or dad doesn't bring home a paycheck, the work they do is super valuable! If they aren't there, the family has to pay for a nanny or babysitter, which costs a lot of money.

data.question: What are liquid assets in estate planning?

options.C: Cash, savings accounts, and life insurance that convert to cash quickly

data.explanation: Assets like cash, savings accounts, or life insurance that can be quickly converted to cash to pay final expenses
data.kidExplanation: Liquid assets are things that can be turned into cold, hard cash instantly. If you don't have cash ready when you pass away, your family might have to sell your house in a panic just to pay the bills!

data.question: Why might life insurance be used for cottage capital gains tax?

options.A: It stops government from collecting tax
options.B: Provides cash to pay capital gains tax, preventing cottage sale
options.C: Forces family to continue living there

data.explanation: It provides the liquid cash needed to pay the capital gains tax, preventing the children from having to sell the cottage to pay the CRA
data.kidExplanation: The government taxes the profit on your cottage when you pass away! Life insurance gives your kids a giant pile of cash so they can pay the tax monster and keep the family cabin.

data.question: What is the Capitalization method for Income Replacement?

options.A: Divide annual income needed by expected interest rate
options.B: Multiply total debt by inflation rate
options.C: Move capital from one account to another

data.explanation: Dividing the needed annual income by the expected interest rate to determine the lump sum required
data.kidExplanation: Capitalization asks a math question: How giant does a pile of money need to be so that the interest it earns every year perfectly replaces the paycheck you used to bring home?

data.question: What is the Capital Needs Approach to life insurance needs analysis?

options.C: Creates comprehensive list of all specific capital needs to calculate exact lump sum
options.D: Assumes money will never be spent

data.explanation: It creates a comprehensive list of all specific capital needs (debts, taxes, education funds, and income shortfalls) to calculate the exact lump sum required
data.kidExplanation: Instead of just replacing a paycheck, the Capital Needs Approach makes a giant shopping list! It adds up the missing paychecks, the funeral cost, the credit card bills, and the kids' college funds to get one perfect total!

data.question: Why is a cash flow check important before recommending Whole Life insurance?

options.B: If no positive cash flow, client cannot afford premium and policy will lapse

data.explanation: Because permanent insurance is expensive; if the client doesn't have positive cash flow (extra money at the end of the month), they cannot afford the premium and the policy will lapse
data.kidExplanation: It doesn't matter if the math says you need a permanent shield if you don't have enough allowance money to pay for it! The agent has to make sure you can actually afford the monthly bill.

data.question: What type of insurance is best for a young family with a tight budget?

options.C: Term Life insurance for highest benefit at lowest initial premium

data.explanation: A Term Life insurance policy, as it provides the highest death benefit for the lowest initial premium
data.kidExplanation: If you need a giant shield right now but you don't have a lot of money, you rent a temporary shield (Term insurance). It's super cheap and gives your family maximum protection!

data.question: What is an exclusion in an insurance contract?

options.B: Specific condition or activity the policy will NOT cover
options.C: Removal of the insurance agent from policy

data.explanation: A specific condition or activity (like suicide within 2 years, or a dangerous hobby) that the policy will NOT cover
data.kidExplanation: Exclusions are the Fine Print. It's a rule that says, We will cover you for EVERYTHING... except if you die doing this one specific dangerous thing, like skydiving.
